title: Basic Search Algorithm
summary:
I'm building a basic search algorithm in Python that allows a user to search an RSS feed from Craiglist. Click here to view the project repo on Github. In this blog post, I explore this project, the challenges I'm facing, and ideas I have for further exploration.

Parameters

I wanted to try my hand at building a basic search algorithm. The following chronicles my progress during the first iteration of this program, the challenges I encountered, and ideas on how to keep improving the program.

The main task was to create a search algorithm that allows a user to do a keyword search of the video games RSS feed from Craiglist for a given city.

Additional parameters for this problem were as follows (in no particular order):

  1. Structure and save the feed data to avoid making continuous calls to Craigslist
  2. Avoid doing a linear scan over every entry from the RSS feed
  3. Support multiple word search queries
  4. Rank the results by relevance

Process

Initial Structuring of Data

My overall strategy was to create a dictionary where I would save important keywords from the RSS feed that pointed to posts where those words were referenced. I assumed that the user would create good, semantic titles; that each post's title woud highlight the content of the post; and that each post's description would be a redundant elaboration of the post's title.

I used the Python package, Feedparser, to fetch the Craiglist video game feed. After saving the feed to a variable, I was able to easily parse out each post, and each post's properites from the RSS feed, such as the title and description. I saved the title, description and link to each post in a list of dictionaries, where each dictionary had the keys "title", "description" and "link" with corresponding values that came from the RSS feed.

Cleaning the Data

I formatted the saved data, removing stopwords (filler words such as "the", "a" and other largely-agreed upon terms) from the title and description. I also filtered out any nonalphanumeric characters, and made all of the characters lowercase. I then split each of the description and title strings into arrays ("lists" in Python), each now containing only the most relevant words from each post. As an example, if the first post had title "Xbox, Gently Used, In Brooklyn," with description "I got my Xbox only a few months ago, but I am moving now and need to sell it.", then I reformatted it something along the lines of the following: [{title: ["xbox", "gently", "used", "brooklyn"], description: ["xbox", "months", "ago", "moving", "now", "sell"]}]

Creating the Reference Dictionary

After cleaning up and getting access to the feed data, I then created a dictionary which contained, keys of each of the words from all of the titles of the posts from the RSS feed. I then set the keys equal to empty arrays. Re-using the example from the previous paragraph, if the title words were "xbox", "gently", "used", "brooklyn", then the first part of the dictionary looked like this: {"xbox": [], "gently": [], "used": []}.

I iterated through each word in every description of the RSS posts. If the word in the RSS post description matched a key in the dictionary (created from the titles of all the posts), then I added the index of that description (its position in the RSS feed) to the dictionary's value. That means that if RSS post at index 3 contained the word "xbox" in its description, then I updated the reference dictionary at key "xbox" to include 3 in its array as follows: {"xbox": [3]}. I also included the the index where the title word was from, just in case the author had not reiterated their title content in their description (an example of this would be a post with title "Xbox for sale!" with no description. Ultimately this left me with a dictionary of all the RSS feed's posts' title words pointing to wherever those words appeared in the RSS feed.

Searching

My search mechanism takes in a series of keywords, and looks up each one in the dictionary I created above. If it finds a match, then it returns the indicies of the RSS feed entries where the keyword is referenced, which allows me to fetch and present those RSS feed entries to the user.

Ranking Results

If the search query word is in the dictionary, then the search function returns an array of indicies pointing to the RSS posts that contain that word. I attempted to assign higher priority to results that were referenced by multiple keywords. For example, if the search query was "xbox", "kinect", the dictionary might have returned indicies 1,3 and 5 for "xbox", and 2,3 for "kinect." Since entry 3 was referenced by both, it represents a union for the results of the query, and is likely to be a closer match to what the user was looking for.

Reflections and Issues

Since I did not want to have to do a linear search over every entry when the user searched, I thought the dictionary would be a good way to have a key list of the major items a person might be searching for. One limitation to this current solution, though, is that this assumes that users are creating good, semantic titles. My current structure might give too much priority to the title words chosen by the user, and also potentially ignores valuable words in the description.

At first I thought might rank entries based on the number of times a query keyword appeared in the entry, but I quickly dismissed this as a largely irrelevant factor for this particular problem. Just because a word is used multiple times in a given entry doesn't necessarily mean that the entry is closer to what the person is searching for; it just means that the language pattern maybe required some repetition. I could see, however, how on a larger website one might need to take into account the number of times a word appears on a page. For example, a website about baked goods might have a random reference/aside to "Xbox", but that doesn't mean that the website's subject is about xboxes, or that this would be a good search result. The weight of the baked goods vocabulary would take precedent in this case, and trump the random apperance of the word "xbox". But for short, and largely to the point entries on Craiglist, I assumed that there wouldn't be a lot of extra references to anything outside the declared subject of the post.

Additional Resources

I often times feel like I am stabbing around in the dark to come up with solutions to problems in programming. While I can use elaborate sets of arrays and dictionaries to mostly manipulate data to my liking, I wish I had better frameworks, even some mathematical principles, to help me solve particular issues. I want to have an arsenal of strategies and algorithms that I can apply to any problem. When I asked my good friend, and also world-class computer scientist, Mike Chow, about this issue, he suggested I pick up O'Reilly's Algorithm's in a Nutshell. Being a fan of the O'Reilly series, having enjoyed their books on Javascript, PHP and CSS, I eagerly purchased a used copy of this book off Amazon, as my local library did not have a copy. I look forward to diving into this book soon, along with Rice University's Coursera Course, Algorithmic Thinking, when it becomes available again later this year.

Conclusion

This blog post was an opportunity for me to sketch out and articulate some of the issues I encountered during my first attempt at building a basic keyword search algorithm. I don't think I have even captured all of the nuances of the project, and I suspect some of my logic might need a second pair of eyes to help me continue to refine my code.

title: On Becoming a Web Developer
summary:
In the Labyrinth

Picture yourself in maze of hallways and doors. Some of the doors are locked and require different keys that you don't have. You pick one of the doors that's already unlocked, maybe "Programming Basics in Python," and start walking down that hallway. This corridor is long and dark, and there are lots of obstacles and problems along the way: broken lights, blocked pathways, all sorts of trick dead ends and tempting distractions, maybe even some sort of monster that's trying to bite you. You have no idea how long the hall goes on for. At some point while still in the "Programming Basics in Python" hallway, you stumble across a door labeled "Objects in Python" to which you don't yet have the key. In order to get the key, you have to go down another hallway labeled "Repetitive And Annoying Tasks in Python that Could Be Solved With Object Oriented Programming To Help One Realize the Importance of OOP" and perform a bunch of tasks there first, but you could also go down another hallway labeled "Javascript." You're tired of Python, so you opt for Javascript, only to discover that the lessons you learn in the Javascript hallway help you get the "Objects in Python" door open anyway.

My experience with self studying web development has been something like this journey described above. I pick a topic and find a learning resource, get stumped or stuck or realize I need to learn something else first, search for other resources, get stumped and frustrated again, switch to other topics completely, and then start the whole messy process over. Often times putting a topic that I'm struggling with on the back burner, and diving into other subjects, has had the unintended consequence of filling in the gaps I needed to master the original topic in the first place. So much of programming is related -- if you can iterate over an array in Ruby, you'll quickly be able to pick up how to iterate over a list in Python, and so forth -- and the skills that you build in one language, framework or area, transfer easily to other areas. The true key is simply properly engaged persistence.

Learning To Be A Web Developer

With increasing confidence, I am able to identify as a "web developer." But how exactly did I get to this point, and how can I keep the momentum going? I decided to reflect on my process for two primary reasons. First, web development's tools and technologies are constantly evolving; it's in my best interest to reflect upon and improve my process for learning new material. Secondly, recording my process and progress might help anyone else who's trying to break into this field.

My study methods have been partly informed by my experience as a teacher. Self studying with the vast variety of online resources has its advantages. For example, I know from brain science that exposing students to multiple resources and perspectives on the same topic helps students retain and apply that new knowledge. Part of the reason for this approach is, simply, we don't know everyone's learning style, or the perfect way of imparting knowledge, and so we opt for variety of techniques in the hope that "something will stick." Even more importantly, though, brain science shows us that observing patterns in a variety of contexts actually helps stimulate the brain and make it retain knowledge in the long-run. (You can find out more information about best techniques for memory retention here). The trick then, is to separate the quality instruction from the rest of the chaff (and there's a lot of chaff out there too), without getting hung up about locating the "perfect" resource.

With all this in mind, here are resources that have been useful to me:

Recommended Resources for Self Study

  1. Codecademy -- Yes, this is pretty standard. This is a very well known introduction to programming, and many first time developers I've interacted with reported having entered the world of self study through this nice and inviting open door. Codecademy provides a great first introduction to the syntax and basic structure of multiple, popular programming languages. By the end of codecademy, you might be able to build some small applications, but it doesn't really teach much programming logic or how a lot of the languages interact to create more complex beasts such as a Ruby on Rails application.
  2. An Introduction to Interactive Programing in Python. While I had taken an introductory computer programming course in college, IIPP was a wonderful re-introduction to the principles of programming. I can't say enough positive things about this course; it was probably one of the best educational experiences of my life and I *highly* recommend it to anyone who wants to learn about programming or just have a good time building some games. IIPP is no walk in the park, however; the first time I took this class, especially during the first introduction to object-oriented programming, I did get stuck. I actually got about halfway through the course, paused for awhile to work on other topics, and returned later complete it a second time around. Right now I'm struggling through the sequel course.
  3. Coderbyte, and Project Euler (named after the mathematician, I believe this is pronounced "Oiler") -- These websites provide problems that can be solved in a variety of programming languages, and are a great way to test mastery of a language. I think the Coder Byte problems are a little easier and less math-intensive than Euler's equations. In an ideal world, I would complete one of these challenges a day. These problems are great, self-contained mini projects.
  4. Books -- Call me old fashioned, but it's really refreshing to get off the computer and crack open a dusty old book, (not to mention, again, that exposure to information in multiple places aids in retention). The O'Reilly series was useful for me for Javascript, JQuery, SQL, and CSS in particular. A lot of these technologies are simple enough to work with so that you can have the book open and basically one window on the computer.

I hope that sharing some of these resources recommendations and reflections might be useful for any developer coming into the scene. My main takeaways are: don't get hung up on locating the perfect study resource, maintain a healthy diet and variety of resources, and stay engaged without being obsessed with mastering everything at once. Perhaps our paths will cross in the maze some day.
